channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: NASA announces astronauts for Artemis III spaceflight, scheduled for 2027 - Al Jazeera
item.description:
1. NASA announces astronauts for Artemis III spaceflight, scheduled for 2027  Al Jazeera

  3. Can NASA Really Land Astronauts on the Moon by 2028?  The New York Times
  4. NASA Marches Toward Artemis III Mission in 2027, Names Crew Members  NASA (.gov)
  5. NASA names 4 astronauts on the 'highly complex' Artemis III lunar training mission  NPR

item.description:

item.title: A lack of sex held back life's diversity for millions of years, fossil study finds - Phys.org
item.description:
1. A lack of sex held back life's diversity for millions of years, fossil study finds  Phys.org

  3. Earth's first animals barely evolved until sex changed everything  ScienceDaily
  4. Lack Of Sex Held Us Back Forever  Science 2.0
  5. Asexual Reproduction Delayed Early Animal Evolution  조선일보

item.title: Astronomers found a galaxy in the throes of death – and they know what’s killing it - The Conversation
item.description:
1. Astronomers found a galaxy in the throes of death – and they know what’s killing it  The Conversation

  3. Galaxy-killing wind discovered in the early universe  The Royal Astronomical Society
  4. Here's Why So Many Massive Galaxies in the Early Universe Stop Forming Stars  Universe Today
  5. Scientists identify a cause for massive galaxies that died young  Earth.com

item.title: New image of the Crystal Ball Nebula captures the ‘spectacular’ throes of a dying star - Yahoo
item.description:

item.title: Only 0.001 percent of the deep ocean floor has ever been seen by human eyes — an area roughly the size of Rhode Island — according to a May 2025 study in Science Advances, meaning more than 99.999 percent of the seafloor covering two-thirds of our pl - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Only 0.001 percent of the deep ocean floor has ever been seen by human eyes — an area roughly the size of Rhode Island — according to a May 2025 study in Science Advances, meaning more than 99.999 percent of the seafloor covering two-thirds of our pl  Space Daily
  2. Ping-pong sponges, ‘black smokers’ and floating somethings: the secrets of the deep sea  The Guardian
  3. 13 Creepiest Things You Can Find at the Bottom of the Ocean  Yahoo
  4. Deepest parts of Earth's oceans are a much more thriving habitat than scientists assumed  Earth.com
  5. Scientists found strange life clinging to rocks in the deepest parts of the ocean  Earth.com

item.title: For more than half a century, theorists insisted that the Milky Way's central black hole had to be exhaling a wind, but the galactic center stubbornly hid the evidence — until a Northwestern team trained ALMA on Sagittarius A* and found a 45-degree cone of mis - Space Daily
item.description:
1. For more than half a century, theorists insisted that the Milky Way's central black hole had to be exhaling a wind, but the galactic center stubbornly hid the evidence — until a Northwestern team trained ALMA on Sagittarius A* and found a 45-degree cone of mis  Space Daily
  2. ALMA Finally Catches the Milky Way's Black Hole “Breathing”  ALMA Observatory
  3. Do black holes do more than devour matter?  Financial Times
  4. The Supermassive Black Hole at the Heart of Our Galaxy Seems to Be Blowing Wind—Just as Scientists Long Theorized  Smithsonian Magazine
  5. Found: Milky Way black hole’s missing wind  Northwestern Now News

item.title: Radio scans find no alien tech from the latest interstellar comet - AP News
item.description:
1. Radio scans find no alien tech from the latest interstellar comet  AP News

  3. SETI Institute Looks for Signs of Technology in Interstellar Visitor 3I/ATLAS  SETI
  4. 3I/ATLAS is only the third confirmed interstellar object ever detected passing through our solar system. It may be older than the Sun itself, and Webb observations show it is carrying an unusually carbon dioxide-rich coma as it swings past once before leaving fo  Space Daily
  5. SETI finds no evidence of alien tech on 3I/ATLAS interstellar comet  AOL.com
